Grab Engineering

Building a real-time event pipeline

This post describes how we built a real-time event pipeline using Apache Kafka, enabling us to process millions of events per second with sub-second latency. The architecture decouples producers from consumers and provides at-least-once delivery semantics.

We chose Kafka over alternatives like RabbitMQ because of its strong durability guarantees and horizontal scalability. The system handles seasonal traffic spikes during promotions without degradation.

Architecture

Our event pipeline consists of three main layers: ingestion, processing, and storage. Each layer is independently scalable. Ingestion uses a tiered approach with edge caching to reduce load on the core brokers.

Processing is done by Flink jobs that consume from Kafka topics and produce enriched events back. Storage tier writes the final state to Postgres for analytical queries and to a columnar store for long-term retention.

Lessons learned

Backpressure handling is critical when downstream consumers slow down. We implemented explicit pause/resume semantics with monitoring. Cardinality explosions in metrics caught us by surprise twice; we now bound dimensions at the source. Retention policies must match data sensitivity to keep storage costs predictable across regions.

The team learned to invest early in observability, especially trace IDs that follow events end-to-end. This paid off when debugging cross-service issues.
